original_abstract: At >95% Coulombic efficiencies, most of the capacity loss for Li metal anodes (LMAs) is through the formation and growth of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI). However, the mechanism through which this happens remains unclear. One property of the SEI that directly affects its formation and growth is the SEI's solubility in the electrolyte. Here, we systematically quantify and compare the solubility of SEIs derived from ether-based electrolytes optimized for LMAs using in-operando electrochemical quartz crystal microbalance (EQCM). A correlation among solubility, passivity, and cyclability established in this work reveals that SEI dissolution is a major contributor to the differences in passivity and electrochemical performance among battery electrolytes. Together with our EQCM, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy results, we show that solubility depends on not only the SEI's composition but also the properties of the electrolyte. This provides a crucial piece of information that could help minimize capacity loss due to SEI formation and growth during battery cycling and aging.
humanized_abstract: Most of the capacity loss at >95% Coulombic efficiency for lithium (Li) metal anodes (LMAs) is attributed to the formation and growth of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI). The underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. In this study, the solution behavior of the SEI in different electrolytes, which is critical for the formation and evolution of the SEI, is systemically characterized and correlated to the passivity and cyclability of the electrodes using in-operando electrochemical quartz crystal microbalance (EQCM) and other complementary techniques such as X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. A unique correlation among the solution behavior of the SEI and electrode passivity and cyclability was achieved, indicating that the dissolution of the SEI in electrolytes significantly influences the passivity and consequently the electrochemical performance of different battery electrolytes. A clear understanding of the influence of the electrolyte on the solution behavior of the SEI as revealed in this study offers valuable insights into the improvement of LMA performance and capacity retention of battery capacity loss during the cycling and aging process.

A unique correlation among the solution behavior of the SEI and electrode passivity and cyclability was achieved, indicating that the dissolution of the SEI in electrolytes significantly influences the passivity and consequently the electrochemical performance of different battery electrolytes. A clear understanding of the influence of the electrolyte on the solution behavior of the SEI as revealed in this study offers valuable insights into the improvement of LMA performance and capacity retention of battery capacity loss during the cycling and aging process.
